content.text: Platform SLA and Support Model for Teams

A Claude developer platform without a defined SLA is indistinguishable from a hobby project. When consuming teams don't know how quickly the platform team will respond to a blocked engineer, whether a production incident will be acknowledged in 15 minutes or 15 hours, or what the availability target for the shared SDK is, they compensate by maintaining private forks and avoiding the platform altogether. A formal support model eliminates this uncertainty and creates the accountability structure that justifies the platform team's existence as a shared service.

attrs.alt: Four-tier support model: Tier 0 self-serve → Tier 1 Slack → Tier 2 ticket → Tier 3 incident

content.text: The support model has four tiers, each with a defined entry criterion, response SLA, and resolution path. Consumers must attempt Tier 0 before escalating to Tier 1, and must attempt Tier 1 before opening a Tier 2 ticket. The only exception is Tier 3 (production incident), which can be opened directly without prior tier escalation.
Tier 1 not resolved in 1 day, or P2+ severity
Production impact, service degraded, team completely blocked
SLAs in the platform support model have two distinct components:
(how quickly the platform team confirms they have seen the issue) and

(how quickly the issue is fixed). Acknowledgment is always faster and always committed. Resolution time depends on severity and effort, so it is a target rather than a hard commitment except for Tier 3 incidents.

The shared SDK and IDP services have an explicit availability target. 'Availability' for a library means that the published version in PyPI is installable, the API has no breaking changes in a released version, and the Secrets Manager integration for key retrieval is operational.
Any team unable to retrieve key > 5 min

The on-call rotation is the backbone of the support model. Every member of the platform core team rotates through on-call weekly. The on-call engineer is the single point of contact for Tier 2 and Tier 3 issues during their rotation and is the backup for Tier 1 escalations that the community cannot resolve.
Monitor #platform-claude-sdk for questions that have no community response after 2 hours.
Acknowledge all Tier 2 tickets within 1 business day and own them to resolution.
Respond to PagerDuty Tier 3 alerts within 15 minutes, 24/7.
Run the weekly health check script (Monday morning) and post the digest to #platform-devex.
Proactively reach out to teams stalled in the TTFD funnel (script runs daily; on-call handles flagged teams).

# PagerDuty on-call schedule configuration (infrastructure-as-code)
schedule:
  name: "Claude Platform On-Call"
  time_zone: "America/New_York"
  layers:
    - name: "Primary"
      rotation_type: weekly
      start: "2024-01-01T09:00:00"
      users:
        - alice@acme.com
        - bob@acme.com
        - carol@acme.com
        - dan@acme.com
      restrictions:
        - type: daily_restriction
          start_time_of_day: "09:00:00"
          duration_seconds: 36000  # 9 AM - 7 PM on weekdays

    - name: "After Hours"
      rotation_type: weekly
      # Same rotation but covers evenings + weekends
      # Only for P1 (production) incidents
      users:
        - alice@acme.com
        - bob@acme.com

escalation_policy:
  - level: 1
    targets:
      - type: schedule
        id: primary_schedule
    escalation_delay_in_minutes: 15
  - level: 2
    targets:
      - type: user
        id: platform_manager
    escalation_delay_in_minutes: 30

At the end of each on-call week, the outgoing engineer hands off all open issues to the incoming engineer in a written handoff note. The handoff must cover: open tickets with current status, any known platform risks or upcoming changes, and any consuming teams that may need proactive follow-up.

## On-Call Handoff — Week of 2025-04-14 → 2025-04-21

**Outgoing:** @alice
**Incoming:** @bob

### Open Tickets
| Ticket | Team | Status | Next Action |
|--------|------|--------|-------------|
| T-234 | search | Root cause identified (API key rotation race condition) | Fix in PR #87, review needed |
| T-239 | ads-platform | Waiting on team to test workaround | Follow up Wed if no response |

### Known Platform Risks
- SDK v2.3.1 has a memory leak in streaming mode under Python 3.12. Patch in progress.
  Teams on Python 3.12: notifications, recommendations. Warn if they report OOM.

### Upcoming Changes
- IDP maintenance window Saturday 2 AM. Notify #platform-announcements Friday.
- SDK v2.4.0 releasing Tuesday. Changelog ready; @carol handles release.

### Teams to Follow Up With
- Team "search": stalled at M2 (first call) for 3 days. DM @dave Mon morning.

A Tier 3 incident is any event where a Claude platform library or service is causing production impact to one or more consuming teams, or where a team is completely blocked from deploying. The incident management process follows the standard ICS structure: incident commander, communications lead, and technical resolver(s).
Alert fires in PagerDuty → on-call engineer acknowledges within 15 minutes.
On-call opens a war room Slack channel: #incident-YYYYMMDD-platform-<short-desc>.
On-call posts initial assessment within 30 minutes: what is affected, severity, initial hypothesis.
If not resolved in 1 hour, on-call pages backup and notifies platform manager.
Affected teams are notified in #platform-announcements: what is down, workaround if available, estimated resolution.
Resolution is posted in the incident channel and #platform-announcements.
Post-mortem is written and published within 5 business days.

## Incident Post-Mortem Template

**Incident:** [short title]
**Date / Duration:** 2025-04-09, 14:22 – 16:47 UTC (2h 25m)
**Severity:** P1 (production degraded, 3 teams affected)
**Incident Commander:** @alice
**Authors:** @alice, @bob

### Summary
[2-3 sentences: what happened, who was affected, how it was resolved]

### Timeline
| Time (UTC) | Event |
|------------|-------|
| 14:22 | PagerDuty alert fires: IDP provisioning portal error rate > 5% |
| 14:37 | @alice acknowledges, opens war room |
| 15:10 | Root cause identified: database connection pool exhausted |
| 16:47 | Fix deployed; error rate returns to 0% |

### Root Cause
[Technical description of why this happened]

### Contributing Factors
[What made this worse or harder to detect]

### Action Items
| Action | Owner | Due Date |
|--------|-------|----------|
| Add connection pool monitoring to Grafana | @bob | 2025-04-16 |
| Increase pool size limit in prod config | @carol | 2025-04-12 |
| Add runbook entry for this failure mode | @alice | 2025-04-16 |

SLA performance is published monthly in the IDP and included in the quarterly DevEx survey results. Consuming teams have a right to see how the platform team is performing against its commitments, and publishing the metrics — including months where SLAs were missed — builds trust rather than eroding it.

# scripts/sla_report.py  — generates monthly SLA compliance report
import pandas as pd
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# Load ticket data from support tool export
tickets = pd.read_csv("data/support_tickets.csv", parse_dates=["opened_at", "first_response_at", "resolved_at"])

# Filter to last calendar month
last_month_start = datetime.now().replace(day=1) - timedelta(days=1)
last_month_start = last_month_start.replace(day=1)
month_tickets = tickets[tickets["opened_at"] >= last_month_start]

# Tier 2 acknowledgment SLA: first response within 1 business day
tier2 = month_tickets[month_tickets["tier"] == 2]
tier2["response_hours"] = (
    tier2["first_response_at"] - tier2["opened_at"]
).dt.total_seconds() / 3600
tier2_compliance = (tier2["response_hours"] <= 8).mean() * 100  # 1 biz day = 8h

# Tier 3 acknowledgment SLA: first response within 15 minutes
tier3 = month_tickets[month_tickets["tier"] == 3]
tier3["response_minutes"] = (
    tier3["first_response_at"] - tier3["opened_at"]
).dt.total_seconds() / 60
tier3_compliance = (tier3["response_minutes"] <= 15).mean() * 100

print(f"SLA Report — {last_month_start.strftime('%B %Y')}")
print(f"Tier 2 acknowledgment SLA (≤1 biz day): {tier2_compliance:.1f}%  (target: 95%)")
print(f"Tier 3 acknowledgment SLA (≤15 min):    {tier3_compliance:.1f}%  (target: 99%)")

if the IDP docs and SDK README don't answer the most common questions, Tier 1 becomes overwhelmed with questions that should self-resolve. Audit the most common Tier 1 questions monthly and add them to Tier 0 documentation.
Slack messages are ephemeral. A question answered once in Slack must be answered again next month. Every recurring Slack question must become an IDP FAQ entry.

a support ticket that receives no acknowledgment in 5 days is worse for trust than one that receives an acknowledgment in 1 day and takes 2 weeks to resolve. The acknowledgment is the promise; the resolution is the delivery.
a single on-call engineer who cannot reach the backup during a Tier 3 incident is a single point of failure. Escalation policy must include a backup and a manager.
a post-mortem that lists action items with no owners and no due dates is not a post-mortem — it is a record that the incident happened. Action items must be tracked in the sprint backlog.

Office hours are a structured Tier 1 support channel that bridges the gap between asynchronous Slack and the overhead of opening a formal ticket. The platform team holds a weekly 60-minute session where any engineer can join, share their screen, and get live help from the on-call engineer. This format is particularly effective for onboarding new teams and for issues that require iterative debugging that is impractical to do over Slack messages.
When: every Thursday, 10:00–11:00 AM (standing calendar invite in the engineering all-hands calendar).
Where: Zoom room linked in #platform-claude-sdk channel description.
Format: open drop-in; no agenda. First come first served, up to 4 slots of 15 minutes each.
Host: the on-call engineer for the week. Backup: the previous week's on-call.
Recording: sessions are not recorded by default (to encourage candid questions). Individual demos can be screen-recorded with the attendee's consent and added to the IDP.

The on-call engineer keeps notes during office hours. Any question that could not be answered by existing IDP documentation is flagged for documentation follow-up. Within 3 business days of the session, the on-call engineer either adds a FAQ entry to the IDP or opens a documentation improvement ticket in the platform backlog.

Office hours are most valuable in the first 30 days of a new team's adoption. The on-call engineer should proactively invite teams that are stalled in the TTFD funnel at M1 or M2 to the next session, rather than waiting for them to find the calendar invite themselves.

Not all support requests are equal. The severity classification system ensures that the platform team's limited attention is directed at the highest-impact issues first, and that consuming teams have a shared vocabulary for communicating urgency.
Platform component completely unavailable; all consumers affected
IDP portal returns 500 for all requests
Production service degraded or team completely blocked
SDK throws unhandled exception in prod; team can't deploy
Significant friction; team productivity reduced but not blocked
Eval runner fails intermittently; TTFD inflated by 1 day
Documentation missing one env var name
Feature request or improvement with no current friction

Consumer teams self-classify severity when opening a ticket. The on-call engineer reviews the classification within 30 minutes and may escalate or de-escalate based on the actual impact. De-escalation requires a comment explaining the revised severity; escalation is done silently.

# Severity classification guide for consumer teams
# Use this when deciding which severity to assign your ticket.

SEVERITY_GUIDE = {
    "P0": [
        "All members of my team cannot use the Claude platform at all",
        "The IDP portal is completely down",
        "All API keys are invalid / returning 401",
    ],
    "P1": [
        "We have a production service failing because of the platform",
        "My team is 100% blocked from shipping a customer-facing feature",
        "A security vulnerability in the SDK is exposing our data",
    ],
    "P2": [
        "We can work but our productivity is significantly reduced",
        "We have a workaround but it is expensive (adds hours per day)",
        "The eval runner is failing and we can't run CI properly",
    ],
    "P3": [
        "I found a bug but have a workaround",
        "Documentation is incorrect or missing something minor",
        "A CLI flag doesn't work as expected but I can do it manually",
    ],
    "P4": [
        "I have a feature idea that would make my workflow better",
        "The CLI could be more ergonomic",
        "I'd like a new example in the docs",
    ],
}

When the platform team misses an SLA — fails to acknowledge a Tier 2 ticket in 1 business day, or fails to acknowledge a Tier 3 alert in 15 minutes — the miss is documented and a root cause is recorded. Repeated SLA misses in the same category trigger a process improvement sprint item.
on-call engineer records the miss and root cause in the ticket. No escalation required.
Second miss in same tier (30 days):
platform PO is notified; root cause review in next weekly team sync.
Third miss in same tier (30 days):
SLA is formally under review; platform manager escalates to engineering VP and a corrective action plan is due within 1 week.

## SLA Miss Record — Ticket T-287

**Ticket opened:** 2025-04-09 14:30 UTC
**First response:** 2025-04-10 17:45 UTC (27h 15m — SLA was 8h)
**Severity:** P2 (Tier 2)

**Root cause:**
On-call engineer @alice was handling a P1 incident (T-284) from 13:00–18:00 UTC
on 2025-04-09. The Tier 2 ticket arrived during the incident response window
and was not visible until the incident was resolved.

**Missed or delayed acknowledgment?** Delayed — no awareness until incident closed.

**Prevention:**
- Add a Slack notification to #platform-oncall when any Tier 2 ticket ages past 4h
  without acknowledgment, regardless of whether an incident is open.
- On-call handoff protocol: if a P1 incident is open, backup engineer monitors Tier 2
  queue during incident response.

**Action item:** ITEM-51 — Add Tier 2 aging alert to on-call tooling (P1, due sprint+1)
For consuming teams that are major users of the Claude platform (typically those with more than 5 active Claude integrations or more than 10% of total API quota), the platform team holds a quarterly

: a 30-minute 1:1 between the platform PO and the consuming team's engineering lead. The agenda covers: what is working well, what is frustrating, upcoming platform changes that affect the team, and any quota or capability needs for the next quarter.

These meetings serve two purposes: they surface high-value friction early (before it becomes a survey statistic) and they build the relationship between the platform team and its highest-leverage consumers. A platform team that knows its top consumers personally is far more effective at anticipating needs than one that relies entirely on ticket data.

The platform SLA and support model is the contract between the platform team and the 30+ engineering teams that depend on its libraries and services. The four-tier structure — self-serve, community Slack, ticketed support, and incident management — ensures that every issue finds the right level of response without overwhelming the platform team. Published SLA targets, a rotating on-call schedule with backup, weekly handoff notes, and monthly SLA reporting make the contract visible and verifiable. Post-mortems with tracked action items close the loop on incidents and drive permanent improvements to platform reliability.
Tier 0 (self-serve docs) must handle 70%+ of questions — invest in IDP documentation before adding support headcount.
Tier 1 Slack: 4 business hours acknowledgment; community + on-call can both answer.
Tier 2 ticket: 1 business day acknowledgment; on-call engineer owns to resolution.
Tier 3 incident: 15-minute acknowledgment 24/7; post-mortem within 5 business days.
Publish SLA compliance monthly in the IDP — including misses and remediation plans.
Every recurring Slack question must become an IDP FAQ entry to keep Tier 1 load manageable.
